food_subgroup: Cooked leafy vegetable dish with ghee tempering

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for Sarson da Saag as a prepared dish; closest match is mustard greens food product (FOODON_03311737). Prepared Punjabi dish context not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers cooked mustard leaves as a vegetable; the full prepared dish with ghee tempering and mixed greens is not individually coded. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for Sarson da Saag as a prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Sarson da Saag is approximately 250 g, served as a bowl of cooked mustard greens curry, usually accompanied by makki di roti (maize flatbread).

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mustard Leaves (cooked) and Spinach (cooked) composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for the complete prepared Sarson da Saag dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition): cooked mustard leaves (sarson), cooked spinach, and ghee entries. The composite reflects approximately 60% mustard greens, 25% spinach, 10% ghee/oil tempering, and 5% aromatics (onion, garlic, ginger). Energy and macros adjusted for ghee addition (~5 g ghee per 100 g dish). Secondary cross-check performed against USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked mustard greens (FDC ID 170402) and cooked spinach (FDC ID 168462).

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — leafy vegetable dishes and cooked greens analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Sarson da Saag in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 32 is estimated by analogy with cooked leafy green vegetable dishes (spinach curry, cooked mustard greens) which consistently show GI values in the 25–40 range in the Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 tables. The low GI is supported by the high dietary fibre content (3.2 g/100 g), low net carbohydrate content (7.5 g/100 g), and the presence of ghee which slows gastric emptying. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (32 × 18.75 g) / 100 ≈ 6.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from cooking salt and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Sarson da Saag provides approximately 775 mg sodium, which represents about 34% of the 2,300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ~32) and high dietary fibre content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sarson da Saag has a low estimated GI of approximately 32 and provides 3.2 g dietary fibre per 100 g, supporting gradual postprandial glucose release; it is a suitable vegetable dish for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-carbohydrate accompaniments such as large portions of makki di roti.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee tempering and sodium load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The ghee used in tempering contributes approximately 3.5 g saturated fat per 100 g, and the sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations; individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion size and request preparation with minimal ghee, while benefiting from the dish's high potassium (420 mg/100 g), dietary fibre, and antioxidant-rich mustard greens.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sarson da Saag is a traditional Punjabi dish from the Punjab region of northern India and Pakistan, made primarily from mustard greens (sarson) slow-cooked with spinach, bathua (chenopodium), and other leafy vegetables, then finished with a tempering (tadka) of onion, garlic, and ginger cooked in ghee. The dish has a deep green colour, a slightly bitter and earthy flavour from the mustard greens, a creamy texture from the blended greens, and a rich, nutty aroma from the ghee. It is traditionally served with makki di roti (unleavened maize flatbread) and a knob of white butter (makkhan). Nutritionally, Sarson da Saag is a micronutrient-dense dish providing significant vitamin A (210 mcg RAE/100 g), vitamin C (18 mg/100 g), calcium (155 mg/100 g), iron (3.1 mg/100 g), and folate (55 mcg DFE/100 g), alongside moderate protein (3.8 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (3.2 g/100 g). The estimated glycemic index is low (~32), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The primary clinical concern is sodium content in restaurant preparations (~310 mg/100 g) and saturated fat from ghee (~3.5 g/100 g), which are relevant for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. In Singapore, Sarson da Saag is served at North Indian restaurants as part of a set meal, typically priced between SGD 13.90 and SGD 16.90, and is popular during winter months in India as a seasonal comfort food.
common_user_queries: Is Sarson da Saag good for diabetics?
What greens are in Sarson da Saag?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for the complete prepared Sarson da Saag dish as served in restaurants. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked mustard leaves, cooked spinach, and ghee entries using a defensible composite ratio. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked leafy green vegetable dishes. Confidence is medium rather than high because exact dish-level data from an approved primary source is not available.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for home-style or restaurant Sarson da Saag based on ICMR-NIN cooked mustard leaves, spinach, and ghee data. Restaurant versions may vary significantly in ghee quantity and sodium. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients except ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero for practical purposes. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero): mustard greens and spinach contain no meaningful vitamin D; ghee contains trace amounts below detection threshold in standard tables. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero): ghee from modern dairy sources contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid is below reporting threshold. 3. The 'Set' designation in the input food name (Sarson Da Saag Set) refers to a restaurant meal set; the canonical food name is Sarson Da Saag. 4. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 A00LC are closest parent matches; no exact prepared-dish codes exist. 5. GI source is analogous estimate; direct measurement study for this dish is not available in the Sydney GI Database as of April 2026. 6. Sodium estimate of 310 mg/100 g reflects typical restaurant preparation; home preparation may be lower (~180–220 mg/100 g).
